coredata.dc:title: Co-Doped S, N-Carbon dots and its fluorescent film sensors for rapid detection of Cr (VI) and Ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Yayun
Zou, Xue
Wang, Wenjun
Wang, Tengfei
Zhang, Shengli
Gong, Zhengjun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) was explored for the identification of Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid (AA), which showed a good selectivity and anti-interfere ability to more than 30 interfering substances. The fluorescence recognition was realized in liquid and solid media under optimal conditions based on the internal filtration effect (IFE). In the liquid medium, the linear dynamic range of Cr (VI) was 0.03–50 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 21.14 nM. The linear detection range and the LOD of AA were 1–1000 μM and 0.28 μM, respectively. For solid-phase detection, we embed S, N-CDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to synthesize a visual fluorescent film sensor, which was irradiated by 365 nm ultraviolet light, for easily and accurately detecting Cr (VI) and AA on-site. The ranges of linearity were 0.1–50 μM and 10–500 μM, respectively, and the LODs were 92.48 nM and 6.99 μM, respectively. This is of great significance such as low cost, user-friendliness, and fast response for the analysis of environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N-CDs/PVA film
Fluorescence quenching
Hexavalent chromium
Ascorbic acid
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S, N-CDs and the film
Characterization of S, N-CDs and film
Optimization of substrate synthesis and Cr(VI) determination
Selectivity and sensitivity test for Cr(VI)
Fluorescence recovery in the presence of AA
Practical application in Cr (VI) and AA determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was backed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21777131 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. A0920502052001-6). We give thanks to the Analysis and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for providing experimental conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart for determination of Cr (VI) and AA by S, N-CDs/PVA film.

(a) TEM image of S, N-CDs. Inset in the upper left corner was the HR-TEM image of one carbon nanoparticle; (b) the size distribution histogram of the S, N-CDs; and (c) SEM micrograph of S, N-CDs/PVA film.
(a) The XPS spectra of S, N-CDs, (b) the FT-IR spectra of PVA film (Ⅰ), pristine S, N-CDs (Ⅱ), and S, N-CDs/PVA film (III).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra and fluorescence excitation spectrum of S, N-CDs/PVA with and without 100 μM Cr(VI). Inset: the photo of S, N-CDs/PVA and S, N-CDs/PVA + Cr (VI) under the sunlight and ultraviolet light respectively; (b) overlap of fluorescence excitation spectra of S, N-CDs/PVA and UV absorption spectrum of Cr(VI); (c) UV absorption spectrum of the S, N-CDs/PVA, Cr(VI), S, N-CDs/PVA + Cr(VI); and (d) the change of fluorescence decay of S, N-CDs/PVA and S, N-CDs/PVA + Cr (VI).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of S, N-CDs with various concentrations of Cr (VI). The inset at the top right was the corresponding photographs for naked-eye observation; (b) the corresponding trend between the logarithm of Cr (VI) concentration and F0/F1-1. The inset at the top left was the calibration curve.

(a) Continuous fluorescence spectrogram of S, N-CDs/PVA film when adding different concentrations of Cr(VI). inset: the photographs for naked-eye observation; (b) the corresponding trend between the logarithm of Cr (VI) concentration and F0/F1-1. The inset at the top left was the calibration curve.
(a) AA (1 mM) turns on the fluorescence intensity of the S, N-CDs/Cr(VI); and (b) relative fluorescence intensity of S, N-CDs/Cr(VI) after adding a series of reducing substances (1 mM).

(a) Continuous fluorescence spectrogram of S, N-CDs/Cr (VI) sensor when adding different concentrations of AA. Inset: derived calibration curve; and (b) continuous fluorescence spectra of S, N-CDs/Cr(VI) film when adding different concentrations of AA. Inset: derived calibration curve.
Comparison of Cr (VI) and AA determination methods based on nanomaterials in recent years.
The actual detection of Cr(VI) in lake water.
The actual detection of AA in commercial lemon juice.

head.ce:title: Co-Doped S, N-Carbon dots and its fluorescent film sensors for rapid detection of Cr (VI) and Ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu 611756, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, China. (Z. Gong)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) was explored for the identification of Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid (AA), which showed a good selectivity and anti-interfere ability to more than 30 interfering substances. The fluorescence recognition was realized in liquid and solid media under optimal conditions based on the internal filtration effect (IFE). In the liquid medium, the linear dynamic range of Cr (VI) was 0.03–50 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 21.14 nM. The linear detection range and the LOD of AA were 1–1000 μM and 0.28 μM, respectively. For solid-phase detection, we embed S, N-CDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to synthesize a visual fluorescent film sensor, which was irradiated by 365 nm ultraviolet light, for easily and accurately detecting Cr (VI) and AA on-site. The ranges of linearity were 0.1–50 μM and 10–500 μM, respectively, and the LODs were 92.48 nM and 6.99 μM, respectively. This is of great significance such as low cost, user-friendliness, and fast response for the analysis of environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As a toxic pollutant, hexavalent chromium is extensively used in leather tanning, electroplating, metal smelting and other industrial processes, which severely threatening the environment and biological systems [1]. If it exists for a long time, it is easily enriched by the food chain. It infects the human body through digestion tract, respiratory tract, skin or mucous membranes, causing ulcers, bronchitis, and rhinitis. Long-term or short-term exposure or inhaling is carcinogenic [2]. As yet, traditional techniques have included atomic/molecular spectrometry [3], flow-injection [4], auger electron spectroscopy [5,6], and chemiluminescence [7]. These methods test directly and the results are accurate. However, most of the instruments and equipment are relatively expensive. The analysis process is complicated and requires a professional operation, limiting its wide application.

Ascorbic acid (AA) is a water-soluble vitamin C with oxidation resistance. As an indispensable trace low-molecular organic compound in the human body, it is widely involved in a series of metabolic reactions in the human body [8]. Since the human body cannot synthesize AA, the AA content analysis in food and pharmaceutical preparations is fundamental. So far, various approaches have been taken advantage of quantitatively determining AA, like chromatography [9], luminescence [10] and capillary electrophoresis [11]. However, limited sensitivity or selectivity and the instrument’s complex requirements restrict the application of some methods in routine analysis. Therefore, researchers have tried to find simpler, more effective, and more ingenious methods to detect samples such as food or water.

In recent years, fluorescence colorimetry based on new nanomaterials has received a lot of attention, since it has the advantages of simple control, intuitive observation, cheap cost, and rapid detection [12,13]. Among them, carbon quantum dot (CDs) has been widely studied in diagnosis [14], sensing [15], imaging [16], and optoelectronic devices [17] because of their excellent biocompatibility, photoluminescence, easy functionalization, inert chemical structure and high-temperature stability [18]. Various types of CDs synthesis methods with advanced features have been discovered, incorporating supported synthesis, arc-discharge, electrochemical oxidation, and combustion, etc. [19]. Besides, CDs are mostly used in liquid form for actual detection, but some CDs solutions dispersed in the solution will accumulate and precipitate over time, thus changing the whole system’s fluorescence intensity. However, some solid-phase materials, such as gel substrate, paper-based sensor, and membrane sensor are stable and can be quickly measured by merely reading the fluorescence intensity of solid samples without the use of reagents and solvents, and will not produce residues [20-22]. Such methods may be more attractive to future micro fluorescence detection equipment and non-professional users.

In this research, a plain, economical, and green method was proposed to prepare strongly fluorescence property S, N-CDs with quantum yield (QY, 17%). It was embedded in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) gel substrate to synthesize a smooth and high fluorescent S, N-CDs/PVA film. We further demonstrated that such S, N-CDs/PVA film can be regarded as a handy fluorescent sensor to determine Cr(VI) selectively. The principle of sensor induction is presumably in view of the construction of a new internal filtering effect (IFE) system, which caused the fluorescence weakening of S, N-CDs by Cr(VI). Moreover, the incorporation of S, N-CDs into the film medium also offered particular hope for stabilizing and immobilizing nanoparticles. The obtained S, N-CDs nanosensor portable film sensor can also detect Cr (VI) residues more intuitively and sensitively. In addition to this, S, N-CDs/ Cr(VI) film can also be used as a nano-fluorescence “on” probe to determine AA. Compared with other AA probes, it has strong points of high selectivity, low cost, and high sensitivity. To prove the fluorescent substrate’s practicality, it was further used to determine them in lake water and commercial orange juice.

Diphosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) and L-cysteine were obtained from Kuer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). PVA, carbamide, NaBH4 and citric acid were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Na2S, Na2SO3, KBr, KI, AA, acrylamide (AM), oxalic acid (C2H2O4) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The Cr (VI) standard solution was prepared with potassium dichromate as raw material. The metal ion solutions used were all made by their nitrates, sulfates or chloride salts. All the solvents used in the experiment were ultra-pure water.

Several types of equipment, including F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK), USB-2000 UV–vis spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USA), JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan), X’Pert PRO X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (PerkinElmer, USA), JSM-7800F scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan), Centrifuge (TD-420, China), Ultrasonic cleaner (GT SonicD9, China), Raman spectra (Princeton Instruments, USA), Vacuum drying oven (DZF-6050, China), and PHS-3 W pH meter (Inesa, China) were applied for the research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S, N-CDs and the film
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of S, N-CDs is approximate to one method which was reported in the literature previously [23]. Briefly speaking, 0.34 g of L-cysteine and 0.15 g of urea was blended with 8.5 g of P2O5, after that 6 mL of water were added to the compound with rapid stirring (Scheme 1 ). Then add different water volumes for dilution to obtain the most suitable fluorescence intensity. Most of the precipitates were separated from S, N-CDs by high-speed centrifugation (4000 rpm, 20 min) and dialysis (molecular weight 1000 Da). The solid carbon points were obtained after freeze-drying for 46 h, which can be used for further characterization.

The S, N-CDs/PVA film was prepared using a straightforward approach. Add 1.2 g of PVA slowly to 20 mL of 3% S, N-CDs solution. The resultant compound was magnetically agitated at 90 °C for one hour to form the homogenous gelatinous liquid and then transferred to a 100 mm petri dish to be horizontally dried 90℃ for 1 h until a uniform and smooth film (thickness about 1 mm) having no viscosity was formed. Then, the S, N-CDs/PVA film was cropped into several 10 × 10 mm rectangles, which corresponded to the solid-state sensing unit of the fluorescence spectrophotometer and ensured that each measurement was in the same position.

All solutions to be detected were quantified at ambient temperature. In the liquid phase experiment, the procedure for fluorescence analysis was performed as follows: 100 μL of PBS buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 7) was mixed with 100 μL of S, N-CDs, followed by adding 1800 μL of various concentrations of Cr(VI) solution. This mixed solution was incubated in a water bath at 55℃ for 3 min andthen used for detection. For solid phase detection, 20 μL of ion solution was added dropwise to the surface of S, N-CDs/PVA film and dried for 3 min at 55℃ to evaluate the fluorescence intensity and spectrum. The fluorescence intensity of the compound was determined by excitation at 362 nm. The width of the fluorescence excitation and emission slit was set to 5.0 nm. The rate of scan was set to 1200 nm/min. For naked-eye colorimetric assays, each corresponding photograph was recorded with a mobile phone.

The relative quantum yield of S, N-CDs sample was determined by contrasting with the prefabricated S, N-CDs with 0.10 mol/L reference fluorophore quinine sulfate (η = 1.33, QY = 54%) [21]. Applying the same, gain, slit bandwidth and excitation wavelength to the above two samples, the QY measurement formula is shown below:
(1)
QY =
QY
ref
η
2
η
ref
2
I A
A
ref
I
ref

where QYref and η are the quantum yield of the reference compound and refractive index of the solvent, respectively; I and A are the integrated fluorescence intensity and the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, respectively. The absorbencies in the fluorescence cuvette whose size was 10 mm were kept lower than 0.05 at λex = 360 nm, and this minimizes the reabsorption effect [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S, N-CDs and film
ce:section.ce:para: According to formula (1), S, N-CDs had a relatively high quantum yield of 17.6%. To further display the shape of S, N-CDs and measure its diameter, the TEM images were acquired. As seen in Fig. 1 a, the S, N-CDs existed in the form of spherical dots and uniformly distributed, the TEM image from one carbon nanoparticle (inset in Fig. 1a) also showed a lattice with an interval of 0.21 nm, on account of the diffraction plane of (sp2) graphitic carbon [25]. A correlation histogram which was obtained by using the Image J software for statistical analysis of about 100 particles was shown in Fig. 1b. Seen from the histogram, it can be concluded that the average diameter of S, N-CDs was about 4.5 nm. The S, N-CDs/PVA film images obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM) in Fig. 1c revealed that the S, N-CDs with an average diameter of 4.5 nm was too small to be observed [22]. However, it can be seen that the prepared film is flat and uniform, which will be more conducive to the reproducibility and accuracy of solid-phase fluorescence detection.

In the carbon dot, nitrogen is a typical dopant, which has five valence electrons and an atomic size equal to carbon.It can act as an n-type impurity, providing excess electrons, causing the Fermi level to move upward, thus changing its optical properties.The doping of sulfur atom is because it can provide energy for the capture of photoexcited electrons or emit trap states, so changing the carbon point pass can effectively adjust the electronic structure and luminous wavelength of the carbon point[26,27]. Therefore, the doping of atoms in S, N-CDs is beneficial to improve its photostability and quantum yield. The surface elements of S, N-CDs were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full spectrum was represented in Fig. 2 a, the values 285.11 eV, 400.4 eV, 531.97 eV and 164.15 eV of the four crests were assigned to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. S, N-CDs was prepared principally involved C (44.17 wt%), N (4.66 wt%), O (17.19 wt%) and S (26.57 wt%), indicating that sulfur and nitrogen were mingled in the interior of S, N-CDs successfully. Notably among them, the C1s spectrum in Fig. S1a showed that values 284.76 eV, 286.02 eV, 287.76 eV and 289 eV of the four crests, belonging to C–C, C-N, C-O and C=O bands, respectively [28]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) showed two crest values at 530.83 eV and 532.35 eV, belonging to C=O and C-O-C/ C-OH groups [29]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) exhibited three crests at 398.88 eV (attributed to C-N-C), 399.77 eV (attributed to N–H) and 400.81 eV (attributed to C3-N) [25]. The spectrum of S2p (Fig. S1d) confirmed the C-S-C group with the presence of two crests at 163.7 eV (related to S 2p3/2) and 164.9 eV (related to S2p1/2) [21]. To sum up, XPS spectra showed that the synthesized S, N-CDs have amino, carboxyl/ carbonyl groups, which are likely to come from the carbon source represented by L-cysteine. The XRD pattern (in Fig. S2a) of S, N-CDs indicated a wide crest centered at 21.87°(0.21 nm), which was due to the high disorder of carbon atoms [30]. The Raman spectrum of the S, N-CDs correspond to the existence of sp3 and sp2 carbons in 1341 cm−1 (D band) and 1568 cm−1 (G band), respectively, with the result represented in Fig. S2b [31].

Fig. 2b shows the FT-IR spectra recorded from pristine S, N-CDs, pristine PVA film and S, N-CDs/PVA film. Pristine PVA film (Fig. 2b-I) showed that the O–H stretching vibrations at 3427 cm−1 [32], and the crest values at 1634 cm−1 and 1383 cm−1 were attributed to C = O and C–H stretching vibration [33]. Similar to S, N-CDs, S, N-CDs/PVA film (Fig. 2b-II and III) exhibited the wide band at 3427 cm−1 appeared due to the O–H/N–H groups [34], C–H stretching vibrations at 2923 cm−1 as well as 2855 cm−1 [32], the characteristic band of N–H and C=O respectively stretching at 1634 cm−1 and 1514 cm−1, the crest at 1383 cm−1 was attributed to the flexural vibrations of C–H, N–H, C-S, and C-N [35,36]. The C-OH and C-O stretching vibrations at 1294 cm−1 [32] and 1112 cm−1 associated [37], and feeble C-S stretching also appeared at 879 cm−1 [35]. Those results demonstrated that S, N-CDs was successfully wrapped on PVA film, which was concurrent with the XPS analysis results.

A strong fluorescence emission crest was centered at 445 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength was at 362 nm in Fig. 3 a. S, N-CDs/PVA film was yellowish under sunlight as the photo I (inset in Fig. 3a), and emitted obvious cyan fluorescence under ultraviolet light (photo III). Then, the word “carbon” was drawn on the film with Cr (VI) solution, and the color change of the whole film was unchanged under visible light (photo II). However, it can be seen that the fluorescence of the depicted position was weakened under the UV light (photo IV). This showed that the introduction of Cr (VI) significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm. Fig. 3b showed that the emission spectra of fluorescence and absorption of UV. The ultraviolet spectrum of Cr (VI) had a strong absorption peak at 362 nm, which overlaps most of the fluorescence excitation spectra of S, N-CDs/PVA. Besides, the change of the absorption peak of the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of S, N-CDs/PVA in the presence and absence of Cr (VI) (in Fig. 3c) also proved that Cr (VI) significantly affected the surface state of S, N-CDs/PVA [25]. In summary, we speculated that the adsorption of Cr (VI) should have effectively shielded the exciting light from the S, N-CDs/PVA film, thus a neo-fluorescence quenching system was constructed [38,39]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifespan of the S, N-CDs/PVA without and with Cr(VI) was metered by the steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer, and the attenuation curve was seen in Fig. 3d. The measured data are given in Table S1, and the following double exponential function (2) can well fit the fluorescence attenuation curve:
(2)
I t
=
A 1
e
( - t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e
( - t /
τ 2
)

among them, τ 1 and τ 2 are the time constants of the two radiation attenuation channels; and A 1 and A 2 are the relevant amplitudes, respectively. Based on the best fitting data, the average lifespan values of S, N-CDs/PVA and S, N-CDs/PVA + Cr (VI) respectively are 7.22 and 7.17 ns, respectively. The slight change in lifespan expresses that there was hardly any obvious electron transfer between S, N-CDs and Cr(VI), indicating that the fluorescence weakening was mainly due to the IFE effect [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of substrate synthesis and Cr(VI) determination
ce:section.ce:para: The dosage of L-cysteine, urea, and P2O5 will influence the S, N-CDs’s fluorescence intensity. This experiment used Box-Behnken Design (BBD) design matrix and multi-response surface optimization to obtain the best fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3). Finally, 0.34 g of L-cysteine, 0.15 g of urea, and 8.5 g of P2O5 were selected to prepare S, N-CDs.

The following series of experiments were carried out to modify the influencing factors like pH value, temperature, concentration, reaction time, and so on. The effect of pH was investigated at first. Fig. S4a showed that the fluorescence has almost no change when the pH was in the range of 3 to 11, indicating that the pH had little effect on the measure of Cr (VI). Besides, to explore the effect of different buffers on the detection, four buffers with pH = 7 were added respectively to determine Cr (VI). The results showed that PBS buffer had the best effect, as shown in Fig. S4b. Similarly, considering the impact of different reaction temperature (25℃, 35℃, 45℃, 55℃, 65℃, 75℃, and 85℃) and corresponding time (1 min, 3 min, 5 min, 10 min, and 20 min) on the detection system, the best incubation temperature was 55 ℃, and F0/F1-1 of the S, N-CDs was unchanged as the reaction time increased gradually (Fig. S4c and Fig. S4d). Aiming at the optimization of solid-phase substrate, Fig. S4e and Fig. S4f showed the optimal dosage of PVA and S, N-CDs during film formation. When the mass concentration of PVA and S, N-CDs was 6% and 3%, the best fluorescence intensity of S, N-CDs/PVA film was obtained. To recapitulate briefly, the above discussion certified the formed by S, N-CDs/PVA coordinating with Cr(VI) was relatively stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity test for Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, it was studied whether other metal ions, anions and molecules affect the fluorescence of S, N-CDs scientifically. As shown in Fig. S5a, when 100μΜ Cr (VI) was added, it was evident that the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CDs decreased significantly. However, there is no obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon when 100μΜ other substances were added to S, N-CDs, demonstrating that S, N-CDs has a fine selectivity. In addition, the effect of higher concentrations of interfering substances on the entire reaction system was also studied (Fig. S5b). Among them, the concentrations of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Li+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and anions all increased 100 times compared with the concentration of Cr (VI), the concentration of Co2+, Fe2+ and Hg2+ increased 10 times, the concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ were equal to the concentrations of Cr (VI). The results showed that the fluorescence signal detected when Cr (VI) coexisted with the above-mentioned interfering substances did not change much. In summary, this method has good specificity and anti-interference ability for Cr (VI), and can meet the high selectivity requirements for Cr (VI) in practical applications.

The fluorescence spectrum changes were observed from S, N-CDs and S, N-CDs/PVA films respectively by adding diverse concentrations of Cr(VI). Fig. 4 a has displayed the fluorescence absorption spectra of different concentrations of Cr (VI). The corresponding fluorescence change photos can be observed under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp when the concentration of Cr (VI) increases (the illustration of Fig. 4a). As observed from Fig. 4b, there was a satisfactory linear relationship between fluorescent intensity decreases (F0/F1-1) and Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 0.03 to 50 μM, the LOD was 21.14 nM when the signal-to-noise ratio was set at 3. The solid-phase fluorescence detection of the S, N-CDs/PVA film for Cr(VI) was shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, the linear range and LOD were 0.1–50 μM and 92.48 nM (signal-to-noise ratio was 3), separately. Compared with liquid phase detection, solid phase detection has a loss in fluorescence intensity and detection limit, but it is still lower than the maximum concentration of Cr (VI) in drinking water specified by the World Health Organization of 50 μg/L (0.96 μM). In addition, it also has certain advantages in portability and stability, which is very suitable for some special environments and on-site measurements by non-professionals. Similar methods for the determination of Cr (VI) and their performance analysis have been listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the analysis method has the advantages of simplicity, rapidness, high sensitivity and wide range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery in the presence of AA
ce:section.ce:para: Since AA plays a significant part in eliminating Cr (VI), the change of Cr (VI) with AA has been widely considered [24]. In this work, AA had the function of turning on the fluorescence signal of S, N-CDs/Cr (VI) in Fig. 6 a. Therefore, under the equivalent conditions, we investigated the effects of some reductants and vitamins (Na2S, Na2SO3, NaBH4, KBr, Riboflavin, Vitamin B12, and AA) on the fluorescence signal of S, N-CDs/Cr(VI), which perceived from Fig. 6b that AA was specific for the fluorescence “on” detection. We speculated as follows: firstly, the Cr (VI) on the surface of S, N-CDs/Cr(VI) nanoprobe may be eliminated due to the chelation of some functional groups in AA, so the fluorescence would be restored when AA existed [24]; secondly, the IFE effect of S, N-CDs/Cr(VI) could be eliminated on account of the reduction of Cr (VI) to a low valence state by AA, the fluorescence of S, N-CDs would then be restored [10].

To evaluate the selective specificity and anti-interference ability of S, N-CDs/Cr(VI) for fluorescence enhancement detection, the fluorescence spectrum of Figs. S6a and S6b showed the influence of some ions on the detection. The experimental results showed that other ions have little impact on the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CDs/Cr (VI). When AA and other larger concentration of metal ions existed simultaneously, the interference of fluorescence enhancement can be neglected, so it can be proved that AA had a good and stable selective fluorescence response. Furthermore, one could well perceive from Fig. 7 a that the fluorescence of S, N-CDs/Cr (VI)/AA mixture increased with the increase of AA concentration. There was a fine equation of regression between F2/F1-1 and AA concentration in the range of 1–1000 μM (R2 = 0.993). The corresponding detection limits were 0.28 μM (at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3). Similarly, when S, N-CDs/Cr (VI) film was used for solid-phase fluorescence detection of AA, a fine linear range from 10 to 500 μM could be obtained in Fig. 7b. When the signal-to-noise ratio was 3, the corresponding detection limit was calculated to be 6.99 μM. Table 1 listed similar methods for the determination of AA and a comparison of their performance analysis. Generally, the above results provide a sensitive and broad detection range for the determination of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in Cr (VI) and AA determination
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate this method’s application value as the Cr (VI) measurement in actual water samples in solid and liquid media, Cr (VI) solutions with different concentrations were prepared from lake water of Southwest Jiaotong University. In accordance with the standard curve and regression equation, the Cr (VI) content in the sample was obtained. As listed in Table 2 , the recovery of Cr (VI) ranged from 95% to 105%. Generally, relative standard deviation (RSD) was ranged from 4% to 8% (n = 3), manifesting that the average recovery and RSD of lake water samples were generally acceptable. In addition, the determination of AA in commercial orange juice by this method was also studied. Foremost, the juice was diluted to reduce the interference of the viscous matrix of massive particles, and then the impurities such as pulp were removed by centrifugation. The content of AA in orange juice determined by S, N-CDs/Cr (VI) sensor was 7.60 μM. Then, the recovery of the method was measured and evaluated by adding a sequence of AA liquids of known concentration. The standard addition recovery and RSD for the determination of AA met the requirements, as shown in Table 3 . Having said all of the above, it is further proved that the monitoring system in actual water and food samples is feasible.

We designed a regulation strategy built on the S, N-doped carbon dots to “switch” the fluorescence determination of Cr(VI) and AA based on the principle of the IFE system. The synthesis processes are simpler and time-saving and provide a sensitive and broad linear range for the determination. Moreover, the solid phase sensor for visible fluorescence detection has been developed by embedding S, N-CDs into PVA thin films to stabilize and fix carbon nanoparticles. In summary, this work provides an effective mean for the development of fine sensitivity, highly selective and portable nanosensors for trace Cr (VI) and AA.

Yayun Ji: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xue Zou: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Wenjun Wang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Tengfei Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Shengli Zhang: . Zhengjun Gong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was backed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21777131), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. A0920502052001-6). We give thanks to the Analysis and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for providing experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106284.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
